title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During a conversation with Jiles and Gwendolen, the gardener Manning recalls the history of Dr. Brent, who resides at Crossby House. Manning discusses Miss Helen Kennedy, the sister of Dr. Kennedy, who returned to the area after marrying an officer from India. Manning recounts two malicious incidents involving Helen: first, an unknown person cut the tennis net at her house into pieces, and later, Helen suffered an injury after tripping on a rake in a dark driveway. The resulting wound on her foot would not heal, which prevented her from dancing.

When Jiles inquires about Jackie Averycliffe, a former employee of the law firm Feen and Worchman, Manning describes him as a "low-class character" and a friend of Helen. However, Manning denies that Averycliffe was responsible for the destroyed tennis net, attributing the act to either stupidity or a way of venting frustration. Manning also mentions Walter Fein, a man who was devoted to Helen but was not loved by her; Fein eventually went abroad and returned to become the head of his firm.

Later, Miss Marple joins Gwendolen and Jiles. She reflects on the nature of unrequited love and jealousy, using the case of Mrs. Erskine and her husband to illustrate how unreturned affection can lead to mutual suffering. Miss Marple cautions that feelings are not always reliable indicators of guilt, noting that people may act contrary to their established characters due to deep-seated motivations or blood ties. She emphasizes the importance of the crime scene and suggests that finding the address of J.J. Avery, the owner of the Water Lily Carriage Company, would be a straightforward task.
